title: #include
description: What the #include preprocessor directive does, angle brackets vs double quotes, why order/indirect inclusion matters, and bits/stdc++.h.
objectives: Understand #include as a preprocessor directive handled before compilation
Understand why you should directly include the header for every feature you use
Understand what #include <bits/stdc++.h> is and why explicit headers are preferred while learning
Trace where #include fits in the full build pipeline

sections.text: 1. What is #include?
#include tells the preprocessor to include the contents of another file before compilation.
The basic structure is #include <header>. Later you'll see headers like <vector>, <string>, <algorithm>, and <unordered_map>.
Do not use those headers yet — we will learn every required header and feature separately.
2. Why Do We Use #include?
Without the required declaration being available, the compiler cannot correctly understand the name you are trying to use. Simple idea: need a feature → include the appropriate header.
3. Why Does #include Start with #?

#include is handled during Preprocessing, not during normal program execution. The program does not reach #include <iostream> while running and decide to include it then — the directive is processed before the actual compilation stage.
# → Preprocessor. When you see #include, think: before compilation.
4. How Does #include Work?

You will often hear "#include copies and pastes the header file" — a useful beginner mental model. More accurately: the preprocessor processes the included file as if its contents appeared at the location of the #include directive.
#include makes the included file's content part of preprocessing at that location.
5. Real-Life Example

sections.title: The report and chapter analogy
sections.text: Your report says "Insert Chapter 2 here." Another file contains the actual Chapter 2 content. Before submitting, someone replaces the placeholder with the actual content: Main Report + Chapter File → Combined Report. Similarly: Source File + Header → Preprocessed Source.
#include = Bring required file content into preprocessing
6. Basic #include Syntax

sections.title: Breaking it down
points: include — the directive requesting file inclusion

sections.text: 7. Two Ways to Use #include
Notice the difference: < > versus " ". Let's understand them.
8. Angle Brackets < >
For beginner-level C++: use < > for C++ Standard Library headers, e.g. #include <iostream>.
9. Double Quotes " "

Double quotes are commonly used for project or user-created headers. Imagine a project with main.cpp and calculator.h — inside main.cpp, #include "calculator.h" means: include our project's calculator.h header.
10. Is the Search Process Exactly the Same?

No. For #include "calculator.h", the implementation commonly searches relative to the current source file or associated local locations first, then configured include paths. For #include <iostream>, the implementation searches its configured system or standard include paths. The exact search rules can depend on the compiler implementation and its configuration.
For interviews and beginner understanding: angle brackets are normally used for standard/system headers, while double quotes are normally used for local project headers.
11. Example Using <iostream>

sections.title: Tracing #include only
points: Step 2 — The requested standard header is processed
Step 3 — Declarations related to standard input/output streams become available
Step 4 — The program later uses cout (accessible as cout because of using namespace std;)

sections.text: 12. What Happens Without #include <iostream>?
We removed #include <iostream>. Now the required declaration for std::cout has not been made available through iostream — the compiler may report an error related to cout.
13. Important: #include Does Not Create cout

Do not think "#include creates cout." Better understanding: the iostream header provides the required declaration for the standard stream object std::cout. #include makes the header content available during preprocessing.

steps: cout exists as part of the Standard Library
Our source can use that declaration

sections.text: 14. Can We Include Multiple Headers?
A C++ program may include multiple headers. But remember our rule: we will not use a feature before learning it.
15. Does the Order of #include Matter?

Sometimes it can. You should not rely on one standard header indirectly including another header you need. Even if code happens to compile in one environment because of indirect inclusions, relying on that is poor practice.
Good habit: directly include the header that provides the feature you directly use.
16. What is #include <bits/stdc++.h>?

bits/stdc++.h is not a standard C++ header. It is commonly available in GCC-based environments and includes a large collection of standard library headers there. Code using it may not compile on every C++ compiler — portability to other compiler environments can be a problem.

During learning, we will prefer explicit standard headers (e.g. #include <iostream>, #include <vector>, #include <algorithm>) so you understand which feature comes from which header. Later, once you fully understand STL, we can discuss when competitive programmers use bits/stdc++.h.
Interview tip: explicit standard headers are clearer and portable.
17. #include and the Compilation Process
#include → Preprocessing Stage. Not execution. Not normal runtime.
18. Dry Run of #include

sections.title: Before program execution, the source file enters preprocessing
points: Step 2 — The iostream header is processed at this location
Step 3 — The preprocessed source goes to the compiler
Step 4 — The remaining build stages occur
Step 7 — The program executes cout << "C++"; → Output: C++

sections.text: #include was handled before the program ran. Runtime execution did not start from #include <iostream> — runtime execution begins from main() for our normal C++ program.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing include without # — include <iostream> is incorrect, #include <iostream> is correct.
Adding a semicolon — #include <iostream>; is wrong, there is no semicolon at the end of a #include directive.
Writing the wrong header name — #include <iosteam> (misspelled) instead of #include <iostream>; spelling matters.
Thinking #include runs when the program runs — it's handled during preprocessing, before the build continues and main() begins.
Using the wrong brackets — standard headers use #include <iostream>, local project headers commonly use #include "calculator.h".
Using bits/stdc++.h without understanding it — if asked why you included it, you should be able to answer; we'll use explicit standard headers while learning.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is #include in C++?
questions.answer: #include is a preprocessor directive that tells the preprocessor to process the contents of another file at the location of the directive before compilation.

questions.question: When is #include processed?
questions.answer: #include is processed during the preprocessing stage, before compilation.

questions.question: Why do we use #include <iostream>?
questions.answer: We use #include <iostream> to make declarations for standard input and output stream features, such as std::cout and std::cin, available to the source file.

questions.question: What is the difference between < > and " " in #include?
questions.answer: Angle brackets are commonly used for standard or implementation-provided headers, while double quotes are commonly used for local project headers. Their file search behavior also differs depending on the implementation.

questions.question: Does #include execute at runtime?
questions.answer: No. #include is handled by the preprocessor before compilation and is not executed at runtime.

questions.question: Is bits/stdc++.h a standard C++ header?
questions.answer: No. bits/stdc++.h is a non-standard header commonly available in GCC-based environments and is frequently used in competitive programming.

questions.question: Should we rely on indirect header inclusion?
questions.answer: No. A good practice is to directly include the header that provides the feature our code directly uses.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: #include is a C++ preprocessor directive used to include another file during the preprocessing stage. For example, #include <iostream> makes standard input and output stream declarations available to the source file. Angle brackets are commonly used for Standard Library headers, while double quotes are commonly used for local project headers. The #include directive is processed before compilation and does not execute at runtime.

sections.title: Trick to Remember #include
sections.text: NEED → INCLUDE → USE (e.g. Need cout → #include <iostream> → Use cout). # = BEFORE COMPILATION. #include → Preprocessor. < > → Standard header. " " → Local header. No semicolon after #include.
